name: Malus (Crab apple) (food medium)

components.name: D-fructose
components.source: USDA FDC 171688 (Apples, raw, with skin) total sugars 10.4 g apportioned by measured Malus sugar ratio; Healthline/USDA sugar breakdown
components.evidence: Fructose is the dominant free sugar in Malus fruit (~57% of total sugars). Crab-apple-specific breakdown is unpublished; apple (M. domestica) proportions used as the Malus reference.

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: USDA FDC 171688 total sugars apportioned by Malus sugar ratio (glucose ~23%)
components.evidence: Second most abundant free sugar; directly fermentable primary carbon source.

components.name: sucrose
components.source: USDA FDC 171688 total sugars apportioned by Malus sugar ratio (sucrose ~20%)

components.name: D-sorbitol
components.source: Aprea et al. 2017 Sci Rep (PMC5359574) 'Sweet taste in apple: role of sorbitol'; USDA
components.evidence: Sorbitol (0.3-0.5 g/100g) is a characteristic apple polyol and a fermentable carbon source; range noted.

components.name: maltose
components.evidence: Minor disaccharide present in apple tissue.

components.name: malic acid
components.evidence: DOMINANT organic acid and a major non-sugar carbon/energy source. Wild Malus mean malic = 11.59 mg/g FW (1.16 g/100g), ~2.2x cultivated apple; crab apples are notably tart. Range across Malus 0.17-2.93 g/100g.

components.name: citric acid
components.evidence: Second predominant acid, PRESENT in wild Malus/crab apples (undetectable to 2.42 g/100g; virtually absent in cultivated apple). Highly accession-dependent, so left unquantified rather than fabricate a mean.

components.name: quinic acid
components.source: Zhang et al. 2018 Metabolites 8(4):74 (PMC6316603); apple food-composition literature
components.evidence: Present in low/trace amounts in Malus fruit; a genuine apple organic acid but not quantified in the crab-apple matrix here.

components.name: succinic acid
components.evidence: Reported at very low or trace levels in Malus; included as present-but-unquantified.

components.name: L-aspartate
components.evidence: Most abundant free/protein-derived amino acid in apple; key nitrogen source.

components.name: L-alanine
components.evidence: Protein-derived amino acid profile of apple.

components.name: L-serine
components.evidence: Apple amino-acid panel. Plant serine is the L-enantiomer (replaces implausible D-serine in the current record).

components.name: total protein
components.source: USDA FoodData Central, Crabapples, raw (SR Legacy NDB 09070 / FDC 171721)
components.evidence: Total protein of crab apple; nitrogen ceiling for the amino-acid pool.

components.name: dietary fiber (incl. pectin)
components.source: USDA FDC 171688 (apple reference; crab-apple fiber not separately reported)
components.evidence: Apple/crab-apple cell-wall polysaccharides; pectin is a fermentable substrate for pectinolytic microbes.

components.name: L-ascorbate (vitamin C)
components.evidence: Vitamin C content of crab apple.

components.name: beta-carotene
components.source: USDA FDC 171688 (apple reference); USDA Crabapple vitamin A RAE 2 ug
components.evidence: Provitamin A carotenoid; source of the 2 ug RAE vitamin A activity in crab apple.

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Single consolidated iron value (replaces duplicated/compartmentalized Fe entries).

components.name: copper
components.evidence: Trace mineral (crab apple is a relatively good Cu source).

components.name: zinc
components.evidence: Trace mineral; added (missing from crab-apple legacy record).

components.name: linoleic acid (C18:2 n-6)
components.source: USDA FDC 171688 (apple reference); crab apple total PUFA 0.088 g/100g (NDB 09070)
components.evidence: Principal apple fatty acid; dominant PUFA.

components.name: oleic acid (C18:1 n-9)
components.source: USDA FDC 171688 (apple reference); crab apple MUFA 0.012 g/100g (NDB 09070)

components.name: total fat
components.evidence: Low total lipid; context for fatty-acid fractions.

remove.name: D-Serine
remove.reason: Plant apple protein/free pool is the L-enantiomer; D-serine is implausible. Replaced by L-serine.

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: Preformed vitamin A (retinol) is an animal-derived form; implausible in a plant food. Vitamin A activity in apple is from beta-carotene, which is retained.

remove.name: Dodecanoic acid
remove.reason: Lauric acid (C12:0) is characteristic of coconut/dairy fats, not apple; apple lipid is dominated by C18 fatty acids. FooDB trace/implausible as a listed lipid.

remove.name: Fe2+ mitochondria
remove.reason: Model compartment/duplicate iron artifact; consolidated into a single 'iron' entry.

remove.name: Iron (Fe3+)
remove.reason: Duplicate iron speciation entry; consolidated into a single 'iron' entry.

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Cobalt trace ion; not a quantified crab-apple nutrient (apple Co is negligible).

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace/contaminant metal, not a growth nutrient of apple.

remove.name: Molybdate
remove.reason: Molybdenum present only at negligible/unquantified trace in apple; dropped as non-quantifiable noise.

remove.name: CO2 CO2
remove.reason: Environmental gas / model boundary metabolite, not a food-composition nutrient.

remove.name: O2 O2
remove.reason: Environmental gas / model boundary metabolite, not a food-composition nutrient.

remove.name: H2O H2O
remove.reason: Water is the matrix, not a compositional nutrient to enumerate here.

remove.name: H+
remove.reason: Proton / pH boundary species, not a food nutrient.

remove.name: Nitrogen
remove.reason: N2 gas boundary species; usable nitrogen is captured by amino acids and ammonium.

remove.name: epsilon-Polylysine
remove.reason: Microbial-fermentation food preservative (Streptomyces), not a native apple constituent; FooDB additive/implausible.

remove.name: (±)-erythro-Isoleucine
remove.reason: Stereoisomer artifact of isoleucine; redundant noise (isoleucine already listed).

remove.name: (R)-Oxypeucedanin
remove.reason: Furanocoumarin characteristic of Apiaceae/Rutaceae (citrus), implausible in apple; FooDB predicted/mis-assigned.

missing_added: malic acid (dominant organic acid, quantified for wild Malus)
citric acid and quinic acid (present, flagged unquantified)
individual fermentable sugars with amounts: D-fructose, D-glucose, sucrose, D-sorbitol, maltose
vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid), B6, folate, vitamin E, vitamin K, choline
L-valine (missing amino acid); L-serine (corrected from D-serine)
quantitative amounts (g/mg per 100g) and mM for all previously name-only components
keep_uncovered: chlorogenic acid (dominant apple hydroxycinnamate; genuine, no BiGG met)
phloridzin / phloretin (phloretin-2'-O-glucoside; signature dihydrochalcone of Malus)
quercetin glycosides (rutin, hyperoside, isoquercitrin; skin flavonols)
sources: USDA FoodData Central, Crabapples, raw (SR Legacy NDB 09070 / FDC 171721) - macros, some vitamins, minerals, fatty-acid fractions
USDA FoodData Central, Apples, raw, with skin (FDC 171688) - individual sugars total, amino-acid panel, B5/B6/folate/E/K/choline/zinc/selenium/beta-carotene
Zhang, Li et al. 2018, Metabolites 8(4):74, doi:10.3390/metabo8040074 (PMC6316603) - organic acids in wild vs cultivated Malus (malic, citric)
Aprea et al. 2017, Scientific Reports (PMC5359574) - apple sorbitol and individual sugar composition
FooDB FOOD00343 (Malus, crab apple) - source record
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources are the free sugars (fructose > glucose > sucrose, plus sorbitol) and, distinctively for a crab apple, a HIGH malic-acid load (~1.16 g/100g, ~2x dessert apple) with variable citric acid - crab apples are the tart, wild-type end of Malus. Detailed sugar/amino-acid/micronutrient values are transferred from the fully-characterized apple (M. domestica) reference because the USDA crab-apple legacy record lacks them; the crab-apple-specific signal is the elevated organic-acid (tartness) fraction.
